title: Legacy
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Legacy
summary: Biblical legacy is not measured by monuments or wealth but by faithfulness — the impact of a life lived for God, transmitted through generations of people shaped by one person's obedience.
body:
Abraham's legacy was a promise: through your offspring all nations on earth will be blessed [verse:gen.22.18]. He died owning almost nothing, but his faithfulness set in motion a story that encompassed the entire Bible.

Moses addressed legacy directly in Psalm 90: teach us to number our days, that we may gain a heart of wisdom [verse:ps.90.12]. Awareness of life's brevity produces intentionality. What do you want your numbered days to add up to?

Proverbs ties legacy to character: a good name is more desirable than great riches; to be esteemed is better than silver or gold [verse:prov.22.1]. The 'good name' is not fame but reputation — the moral residue left behind when you leave a room or a life.

Hebrews 11 catalogs the legacies of faith's heroes. Many of them — Abel, Enoch, Abraham, Sarah — did not receive the full promise in their lifetimes but were commended for their faith [verse:heb.11.13,heb.11.39]. Legacy in Scripture often outlives the person who builds it.

Paul knew his end was near when he wrote to Timothy: I have fought the good fight, I have finished the race, I have kept the faith [verse:2tim.4.7]. His legacy was not buildings or institutions but a life of faithfulness and the people he had poured into.

Timothy himself was legacy in action — the fruit of Paul's investment, carrying the gospel into the next generation [verse:2tim.2.2]. The best legacy is not what you leave behind but who you leave behind.

verses.annotation: Abraham's legacy: all nations blessed through his faithfulness.
Number your days — urgency produces wisdom and intentional living.
A good name over riches — legacy as moral character, not material accumulation.
Faith heroes who died without receiving the promise — legacy beyond one's lifetime.
Paul's final self-assessment: faithfulness, not fame.
Entrust to faithful people who will teach others — legacy is multiplication.

scholars.quote: Sarna notes that God's promise to Abraham in Genesis 22:18 reframes legacy from biological succession to universal blessing — Abraham's significance lies not in his descendants' number but in the blessing they mediate.
VanGemeren reads Psalm 90:12 as Moses' prayer for perspective — numbering one's days is not morbid but liberating, producing the wisdom to invest in what truly lasts.

Schreiner argues that Paul's charge to Timothy in 2 Timothy 2:2 establishes a four-generation chain — Paul to Timothy to faithful people to others also — making legacy not a single act but a self-perpetuating process.
